title: PacketForge Lambda Lockout: The Missing Permission
summary: A threat intelligence pipeline that ran for months starts failing overnight. The code hasn't changed, but something else did.

personality.demeanor: direct and technical, frustrated by what should have been a routine deployment
personality.recurring_concern: enterprise customer churn, Sentinel plan SLA breaches

facts: threat-feed sync has not run since the deployment
enterprise customers on Sentinel plan seeing stale threat intelligence data
three customers have SLAs guaranteeing updates within 15 minutes
Ridgeline Financial (largest enterprise account, $480K ARR) opened P1 support ticket
their security operations center relies on threat feeds for real-time blocking rules
Lambda function code is correct -- worked fine in staging
difference must be in the execution environment, not the code
check what permissions the function has in production
Lambda function is invoking correctly and DynamoDB table exists
issue is not connectivity or code -- it is authorization
check what the execution role allows this function to do

hints.hint: error message says AccessDeniedException -- what controls whether a Lambda function can access other AWS services?
Lambda functions use an IAM execution role to interact with other AWS services -- check the permissions attached to this function's role
execution role has DynamoDB permissions, but check the specific actions -- does it allow the operations the function is trying to perform?
role allows dynamodb:GetItem and dynamodb:Query but function also needs dynamodb:PutItem and dynamodb:UpdateItem -- those actions are missing from the policy

glossary.Execution Role: An IAM role assigned to a Lambda function that defines which AWS services and resources the function is allowed to access. Without it, the function cannot call any other AWS API.
glossary.IAM Policy: A JSON document that lists specific actions (like dynamodb:PutItem) a user or role is allowed or denied. Policies are attached to roles or users to grant permissions.
glossary.Least Privilege: A security principle where you grant only the exact permissions needed and nothing more. If a permission is not explicitly allowed, it is denied by default.
glossary.AccessDeniedException: An error returned by AWS when a request tries to perform an action that the caller's IAM policy does not allow. It means the permission is missing, not that the resource is gone.
glossary.Trust Policy: A special IAM policy on a role that says which service (like lambda.amazonaws.com) is allowed to assume that role. It does not grant permissions to AWS resources directly.
glossary.DynamoDB Action: A specific operation you can perform on a DynamoDB table, such as GetItem (read one row), PutItem (write one row), or Query (search by key). Each action must be separately allowed in an IAM policy.
glossary.ARN: Amazon Resource Name. A unique identifier for any AWS resource, such as a specific DynamoDB table or IAM role. Policies use ARNs to scope permissions to exact resources.
glossary.CloudWatch Logs: A service that collects and stores log output from Lambda functions and other AWS services. Error messages like AccessDeniedException appear here.
glossary.BatchWriteItem: A DynamoDB operation that writes up to 25 items in a single API call. It is a different action from PutItem and requires its own IAM permission.

narrative_arc.call: alert arrives, stakes established from story.md Opening
narrative_arc.threshold: player opens CloudWatch logs, sees AccessDeniedException errors -- function fetches data successfully but fails on every write to DynamoDB
narrative_arc.trials: Lambda code correct and worked in staging, DynamoDB table exists and healthy, function invokes on schedule -- red herring is assuming problem is in code, table, or connectivity
narrative_arc.revelation: IAM execution role only allows GetItem, Query, and now-unused BatchWriteItem -- refactored code uses PutItem and UpdateItem which were never added to the policy
narrative_arc.return: add missing permissions to execution role, remove unused BatchWriteItem, add IAM policy simulation step to CI/CD pipeline

components.name: packetforge-threat-sync (Lambda function)
components.role: Pulls threat data from 14 external feeds every 5 minutes, enriches it, and writes results to DynamoDB.
connections: Triggered by EventBridge rule on 5-minute schedule
Reads and writes to packetforge-threats DynamoDB table
components.failure_impact: If this function fails, threat intelligence data goes stale and enterprise customers receive outdated threat feeds.

components.name: packetforge-threat-sync-role (IAM execution role)
components.role: Grants the Lambda function permission to call specific AWS APIs. Defines what the function is allowed to do.
connections: Policy scoped to packetforge-threats DynamoDB table and CloudWatch Logs
components.failure_impact: If the role's policy is missing required actions, the function receives AccessDeniedException on every disallowed API call.

components.name: packetforge-threats (DynamoDB table)
components.role: Stores enriched threat intelligence data. Serves as the backing store for the customer-facing API.
connections: Written to by packetforge-threat-sync Lambda function
Read by the customer API running on ECS Fargate
components.failure_impact: If writes fail, the table serves stale data. The table itself remains healthy -- the problem is upstream authorization.

components.name: CloudWatch Logs and Metrics
components.role: Captures Lambda invocation logs and error metrics. The error rate alarm fires when invocations fail.
connections: Receives logs from packetforge-threat-sync Lambda function
Error alarm triggers on non-zero error count
components.failure_impact: If logging permissions were also missing, the team would have no visibility into why the function fails.

system_narration.data_flow: EventBridge schedule -> Lambda function fetches from external threat feeds -> Lambda enriches data -> Lambda writes to DynamoDB table -> Customer API reads from DynamoDB -> Enterprise customers
system_narration.what_broke: The IAM execution role's policy allowed GetItem, Query, and BatchWriteItem. The code refactor replaced BatchWriteItem with PutItem and UpdateItem, but nobody updated the IAM policy. Production's least-privilege role correctly denied the unauthorized actions.

resolution.root_cause: The Lambda function packetforge-threat-sync's IAM execution role only grants dynamodb:GetItem and dynamodb:Query permissions. After a code refactor, the function now also requires dynamodb:PutItem and dynamodb:UpdateItem to write threat feed data to DynamoDB, but these actions were never added to the execution role policy.

fix_criteria.description: Player identifies that the Lambda execution role is missing dynamodb:PutItem and dynamodb:UpdateItem permissions
Player connects the issue to the recent code deployment that added write operations not covered by the existing IAM policy
Player proposes adding the missing DynamoDB write permissions to the execution role's IAM policy

learning_objectives: Lambda functions use IAM execution roles to interact with other AWS services -- the role's policies define what the function can do
IAM policies follow the principle of least privilege -- only the explicitly allowed actions are permitted
When deploying code changes that interact with new AWS API actions, the execution role must be updated to match
sop_steps: Start by reading the error logs. Open CloudWatch Logs (where Lambda sends its output) for the failing function. Look for AccessDeniedException -- this error means the function tried to do something it does not have permission for. The log message will tell you exactly which action was denied.

Find out which permissions the function has. Every Lambda function runs with an IAM execution role -- a set of permissions that controls what AWS services the function can access. Use get_function_configuration to find the role's ARN (Amazon Resource Name -- the unique identifier for the role).

Look at what the role is actually allowed to do. Use list_attached_role_policies and get_role_policy to pull up the IAM policies (JSON documents that list permitted actions) attached to the execution role.

Compare the list of allowed actions in the policy to the actions the function code actually needs. The function calls PutItem and UpdateItem (to write data to DynamoDB), plus GetItem and Query (to read data). Check whether all four are listed in the policy.

Identify the gap: dynamodb:PutItem and dynamodb:UpdateItem are missing from the policy. The function is trying to write data, but the policy only allows reading. That is why every write fails with AccessDeniedException.

Add the missing permissions. Update the IAM policy to include dynamodb:PutItem and dynamodb:UpdateItem. Scope these permissions to the specific DynamoDB table by using its ARN (arn:aws:dynamodb:us-east-1:672913845210:table/packetforge-threats) -- this follows the principle of least privilege, granting access only to the exact resource needed.

Clean up old permissions. Remove dynamodb:BatchWriteItem from the policy since the refactored code no longer uses that action. Keeping unused permissions around increases security risk for no benefit.
Test the fix. Wait for the next scheduled run (or trigger the function manually) and check CloudWatch Logs to confirm the function completes without errors.
Verify end-to-end by checking that the DynamoDB write count metric in CloudWatch returns to a non-zero value. This confirms data is flowing into the table again.

related_failure_modes.scenario: Lambda function has the correct IAM permissions but the DynamoDB table uses an encryption key the execution role cannot access
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: The DynamoDB permissions look correct, but the table is encrypted with a customer-managed KMS key (KMS is the Key Management Service -- it manages encryption keys). The function gets AccessDeniedException on the encryption operation (kms:Decrypt or kms:GenerateDataKey) rather than on the DynamoDB action itself. This is confusing because the error happens during a DynamoDB call but is actually a key-access problem.
related_failure_modes.prevention: When a DynamoDB table uses a customer-managed encryption key, add kms:Decrypt and kms:GenerateDataKey permissions to the execution role, scoped to that specific key.

related_failure_modes.scenario: An organization-level policy overrides the function's permissions and blocks DynamoDB writes
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: The function's own permissions look correct, but a Service Control Policy (SCP) -- a rule set at the AWS Organization level that applies to all accounts -- explicitly blocks DynamoDB writes in the production account. SCPs act like a ceiling on permissions: even if a role allows an action, an SCP can deny it from above. This makes the problem invisible when you only inspect the role.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Before deploying, review SCPs in your AWS Organization. Use iam:SimulatePrincipalPolicy (a tool that checks whether a role can actually perform an action, considering all policy layers) against the production role to test effective permissions end-to-end.

related_failure_modes.scenario: The scheduling service loses permission to trigger the Lambda function after a policy change
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: The function never runs at all -- CloudWatch Logs show zero invocations, not an AccessDeniedException during execution. The issue is that EventBridge (the scheduling service) lost permission to invoke the function, which is a different kind of authorization. The function's own execution role is fine; the problem is who is allowed to start the function.
related_failure_modes.prevention: After changing a Lambda function's resource-based policy (the policy that controls who can trigger the function), verify that the event source can still invoke it by sending a test event.

related_failure_modes.scenario: The IAM policy has a condition that does not match the Lambda execution environment
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: The actions are listed as allowed in the policy, but a condition limits when they apply. For example, a condition like aws:SourceVpc restricts the action to calls from a specific virtual network -- but Lambda functions do not always run inside a VPC. The policy appears to allow the action, but the condition silently blocks it. Testing with SimulatePrincipalPolicy without providing the right context would show 'Allow', hiding the real issue.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Avoid adding VPC or IP-based conditions to Lambda execution roles unless the function actually runs inside a VPC. When testing policies, always include realistic context values so the simulation matches real-world behavior.

sop_practices: Before deploying, test whether the production role actually has the permissions your code needs. Use iam:SimulatePrincipalPolicy -- a tool that checks whether a role is allowed to perform specific actions -- for every DynamoDB action the code calls. Add this check as a required gate in your deployment pipeline (CI/CD).

Use the same tightly scoped permissions in staging as in production. Never use wildcard permissions like dynamodb:* (meaning 'allow all DynamoDB actions') in staging, because it hides permission gaps that will only surface when you deploy to production.

When you change your code to call different AWS actions than before, update the IAM policy in the same pull request. Treat the permissions as part of the code change, not a separate task -- otherwise it is easy to forget and ship broken code.

Set up a CloudWatch alarm on the Lambda Errors metric with a threshold of 1 and a 1-minute evaluation period. This means you will be alerted within one invocation cycle if the function starts failing, whether from permission errors or anything else.
